questions.question: Explain the need for virtual destructors in C++ with an example.
keyPoints: Virtual destructors ensure proper cleanup when deleting derived objects through base class pointers
Without virtual destructor, only base class destructor executes
This leads to memory leaks in derived class resources
Example: Base* ptr = new Derived(); delete ptr; // Without virtual: only Base destructor called

questions.question: What is destructor chaining? Explain the order of destructor calls in inheritance.
keyPoints: Destructor chaining is automatic invocation of base class destructor after derived class destructor
Order: Derived class destructor executes first, then base class destructor
This follows reverse order of constructor execution
Ensures proper cleanup of resources in correct dependency order

questions.question: Can destructors be overloaded? Justify your answer.
keyPoints: No, destructors cannot be overloaded
They have no return type and no parameters
Only one destructor per class is allowed
If not defined, compiler generates implicit destructor

questions.question: What happens if a destructor throws an exception in C++?
keyPoints: Destructors should not throw exceptions
If destructor throws during stack unwinding, std::terminate() is called
This terminates the program immediately
Use try-catch internally if cleanup might fail, never propagate exceptions

questions.question: Explain the difference between private and protected destructors with use cases.
keyPoints: Private destructor: Objects can only be created on heap, cannot use delete on objects from outside class
Protected destructor: Allows derived classes to call base destructor
Private useful for singleton pattern or custom memory management
Protected useful when derived classes need to destroy base subobjects

questions.question: What is a pure virtual destructor? When and why is it used?
keyPoints: Pure virtual destructor declared with = 0: virtual ~BaseClass() = 0;
Must provide implementation (definition) for pure virtual destructor
Makes class abstract while ensuring proper cleanup in derived classes
Used when you want abstract base class but need virtual destructor for polymorphic deletion

questions.question: Write a C++ program demonstrating destructor execution order in single inheritance.
keyPoints: Create base and derived class with cout in constructors and destructors
Show derived object construction order: Base→Derived
Show destruction order: Derived→Base
Code should clearly display execution sequence to demonstrate chaining

questions.question: Design a class hierarchy for a FileSystem with Base class File and derived classes TextFile and BinaryFile. Explain how virtual destructors ensure proper resource cleanup when files are opened/closed through base class pointers.
keyPoints: Base class File with name, handle attributes and virtual destructor
Derived TextFile with buffer, BinaryFile with binary data members
Virtual destructor in File ensures proper cleanup of derived members
Show polymorphism: File* ptr = new TextFile(); delete ptr; // calls both destructors
Explain memory leak prevention and resource release order

questions.question: Explain multiple inheritance and virtual destructor interactions. Create a scenario with base classes A and B, derived class C, and demonstrate the destructor call order when deleting through different base pointers.
keyPoints: When using multiple inheritance, each base class that may be polymorphic needs virtual destructor
Destructor order: C's destructor → B's destructor → A's destructor (reverse of declaration)
When deleting through A* or B* pointers, only that base's destructor is called unless virtual
Show code example with A, B, C classes and demonstrate deletion scenarios
Explain importance of virtual destructors in all base classes for proper cleanup

questions.question: Compare and contrast constructors and destructors in C++ with respect to: a) Execution order in inheritance b) Parameter passing c) Overloading capability d) Virtual behavior
keyPoints: a) Constructors: Base→Derived, Destructors: Derived→Base (reverse order)
b) Constructors can have parameters, destructors cannot
c) Constructors can be overloaded, destructors cannot
d) Virtual constructors don't exist, virtual destructors enable polymorphic cleanup
e) If constructor throws, destructor not called for that object
f) Destructors should be virtual for polymorphic base classes

questions.question: Write a complete C++ program demonstrating RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization) principle using a class that manages a dynamic array. Show how destructor ensures no memory leak occurs.
keyPoints: Create class DynamicArray with dynamic memory allocation in constructor
Destructor frees the allocated memory
Show proper cleanup when object goes out of scope
Demonstrate RAII: resource lifetime tied to object lifetime
Add copy constructor and assignment operator to handle deep copy properly
Show that no explicit delete is needed - destructor handles cleanup
